other_observers: Susan Allerton and Barb Byron; Richard Brown was the original observer and he lives at the above address. The first person to correctly identify the bird was Andrew Spencer, as reported to me by Susan Allerton. Many people observed the bird including Andrew Boyce, Nathan Pieplow,
specific_location: Durango Mountain Resort at 322 Sheol Road Unit #6.
description: This was a large female adult hummingbird. It was about 25% longer than a Broad-tailed. The bill was long, straight and dark. Overall the bird was green on the back and rump and grayish on the underparts. There was a prominent post ocular spot that extended back from the eye in a fine white line. There was also a faint whitish area from below the bill to just past the eye, but this mark was difficult to see and not sharply defined. The throat was very finely spotted and could only be discerned with a scope at about 75 feet. The flanks were lightly washed with greenish-gray. The tail was fairly large and dark underneath. The outer rectices were tipped with white and the white seemed more extensive underneath than on top. The rump was green and this extended well into the tail.Behaviors: The first time I saw this bird (of three sightings) it was confronted by a Rufous and it fanned it tail broadly and rapidly several times. I did not see this tail fanning behavior in subsequent sightings. The wing beats were slow and visible, rather than the blur of most hummingbirds. The bird was an infrequent visitor to one set of nectar feeders and despite many observers, we could not locate where this bird went in its long periods between visits. Even then, visits at the feeders were short.Call: Not heardPlumage: Adult
